sinus_normal.label: Normal sinus rhythm
sinus_normal.p: One upright P before every QRS, same shape each time
sinus_normal.hallmark: The baseline everything else is judged against. P before every QRS, QRS after every P, steady spacing.
sinus_normal.mnemonic: "P before Q, in lockstep" — if the Ps and QRSs march together at a normal rate, you're home.
sinus_normal.confuse: Sinus tachycardia and sinus bradycardia — same anatomy, only the rate changes.
sinus_normal.action: No treatment. This is your reference strip — learn its proportions cold.

sinus_brady.label: Sinus bradycardia
sinus_brady.hallmark: Normal sinus rhythm, just slow. Everything is present and married — there's simply more baseline between beats.
sinus_brady.mnemonic: "Sinus, but sleepy." All the parts, extra spacing.
sinus_brady.confuse: Junctional rhythm (no/inverted P) and heart blocks (P–QRS relationship broken). In sinus brady the relationship is intact.
sinus_brady.action: Only treat if symptomatic: atropine 1 mg → pacing/pressors per the Bradycardia algorithm.

sinus_tach.label: Sinus tachycardia
sinus_tach.p: One P before every QRS — may start merging into the preceding T at high rates
sinus_tach.hallmark: A normal rhythm responding to a stressor — fever, pain, hypovolemia, hypoxia, anxiety. It speeds up and slows down gradually.
sinus_tach.mnemonic: "Sinus tach has a story." There's always a reason — find and treat the cause, not the rate.
sinus_tach.confuse: SVT. Sinus tach: usually < 150, P waves visible, gradual onset, a cause you can name. SVT: usually > 150, P waves buried, flips on like a light switch.
sinus_tach.action: Treat the underlying cause. Do not cardiovert a sinus tachycardia.

svt.label: Supraventricular tachycardia
svt.p: Not visible — buried in the preceding T wave
svt.hallmark: Abrupt-onset, very fast, very regular narrow-complex rhythm with no visible P waves. A re-entry loop, not a stressed sinus node.
svt.mnemonic: "Fast, flat-out, and P-less." Regular + narrow + no P = SVT.
svt.confuse: Sinus tachycardia (has P waves and a story) and atrial flutter 2:1 (look for sawtooth between QRSs, rate suspiciously close to 150).
svt.action: Stable: vagal maneuvers → adenosine 6 mg → 12 mg. Unstable: synchronized cardioversion at 100 J.

afib.label: Atrial fibrillation
afib.regular: Irregularly irregular — no pattern to the spacing at all
afib.p: None — replaced by a chaotic, wavering fibrillatory baseline
afib.hallmark: The R waves land unpredictably, like rain on a roof. The baseline quivers instead of showing P waves.
afib.mnemonic: "Irregularly irregular = AFib until proven otherwise." If you can't tap your foot to it, it's fibbing.
afib.confuse: MAT (has P waves, ≥3 shapes) and sinus arrhythmia (has P waves, gently cyclic). AFib has no P waves at all.
afib.action: Stable: rate control (β-blocker/CCB), consider anticoagulation. Unstable: synchronized cardioversion at 200 J.

aflutter.label: Atrial flutter
aflutter.rate: Atrial ≈ 300; ventricular depends on conduction ratio — 2:1 gives ≈ 150
aflutter.p: No true Ps — continuous sawtooth flutter waves instead
aflutter.hallmark: The baseline never rests — it's a continuous sawtooth. A ventricular rate pinned near exactly 150 should always make you hunt for buried flutter waves.
aflutter.mnemonic: "Sawtooth = flutter. And 150 on the dot? Suspect flutter 2:1."
aflutter.confuse: SVT at ~150 bpm — the classic trap. Look between the QRS complexes for teeth.
aflutter.action: Managed like AFib: rate control if stable, synchronized cardioversion at 200 J if unstable.

vt_mono.label: Monomorphic ventricular tachycardia
vt_mono.qrs: Wide (≥ 0.12 s) — every complex the same shape
vt_mono.hallmark: Wide, regular, all-identical complexes at speed. The ventricle is driving, so nothing looks crisp — every beat is a broad paintbrush stroke.
vt_mono.mnemonic: "Wide = ventricle, until proven otherwise." Same shape every beat = MONOmorphic.
vt_mono.confuse: SVT with aberrancy. The safe default — on the exam and at the bedside — is to treat wide-regular as VT.
vt_mono.action: Pulse + stable: antiarrhythmic infusion, expert consult. Pulse + unstable: synchronized cardioversion 100 J. No pulse: defibrillate — it's the arrest algorithm.

torsades.label: Torsades de pointes
torsades.qrs: Wide, with amplitude that grows and shrinks in a spindle pattern
torsades.hallmark: Polymorphic VT that "twists around the points" — the complexes swell and shrink like a party streamer. Classically from long QT (drugs, low K⁺/Mg²⁺).
torsades.confuse: Coarse VF (pure chaos, no twisting envelope) and monomorphic VT (constant amplitude).
torsades.action: Magnesium 1–2 g IV. Pulseless: defibrillate — never synchronized cardioversion (no reliable R to sync on).

vf_coarse.label: Coarse ventricular fibrillation
vf_coarse.rate: None — no organized beats to count
vf_coarse.hallmark: Big, erratic squiggles with zero repeating structure. No P, no QRS, no pattern — the ventricle is quivering, not beating.
vf_coarse.mnemonic: "If there's no rhythm to name, it's fibrillation." Coarse VF = fresh VF = most shockable.
vf_coarse.confuse: Torsades (has a twisting envelope) and artifact (check the patient — artifact happens on a patient with a pulse).
vf_coarse.action: Defibrillate immediately, 120–200 J biphasic, then CPR. Sync OFF.

vf_fine.label: Fine ventricular fibrillation
vf_fine.hallmark: VF that has been running long enough to run out of energy — a shallow, trembling line. One monitor-gain click away from looking like asystole.
vf_fine.mnemonic: "Fine VF is VF running out of battery." Before calling a flat line asystole: check leads, check gain, check a second lead.
vf_fine.confuse: Asystole — the critical lookalike. Turning up the gain or switching leads can reveal fibrillation you can still shock.
vf_fine.action: Still shockable — defibrillate, high-quality CPR, epinephrine per the arrest algorithm.

asystole.label: Asystole
asystole.p: None (rarely, P waves persist alone — "P-wave asystole")
asystole.hallmark: A nearly flat line with only gentle baseline drift. Confirm it's real before you treat it: leads attached? Gain up? Second lead checked?
asystole.mnemonic: "Flat? Confirm before you call it" — leads, gain, second lead.
asystole.confuse: Fine VF (shockable!) and a disconnected lead (the patient with a pulse and a flat monitor is a connection problem).
asystole.action: Not shockable. CPR + epinephrine ASAP, hunt H's & T's hard.

first_degree.label: 1st-degree AV block
first_degree.rate: Whatever the underlying sinus rate is (this strip ≈ 75)
first_degree.p: One P before every QRS — none dropped
first_degree.hallmark: Every P still gets through — it just takes too long. A fixed, stretched PR on every single beat.
first_degree.mnemonic: "If the R is far from P, then you have a FIRST degree."
first_degree.confuse: Normal sinus (measure the PR — more than one big box = prolonged).
first_degree.action: Usually none. Recognize it, monitor, review AV-nodal-blocking meds.

mobitz1.label: 2nd-degree AV block, Mobitz I (Wenckebach)
mobitz1.regular: Regularly irregular — a repeating pattern of grouped beats
mobitz1.p: More Ps than QRSs; the P–QRS relationship stretches then snaps
mobitz1.pr: Progressively lengthens beat by beat, then a QRS is dropped, then the cycle restarts
mobitz1.hallmark: PR stretches… stretches… stretches… drop. Then it resets and does it again. The dropped beats come in a predictable cycle.
mobitz1.mnemonic: "LONGER, longer, longer, DROP — then you have a WENCKEBACH."
mobitz1.confuse: Mobitz II — there the PR never changes; beats just vanish without warning. Wenckebach warns you first.
mobitz1.action: Usually benign, often vagal/nodal. Treat only if symptomatic; atropine typically works (block is at the AV node).

mobitz2.label: 2nd-degree AV block, Mobitz II
mobitz2.regular: Irregular where beats drop; conducted beats keep a fixed PR
mobitz2.p: More Ps than QRSs; dropped QRSs come without warning
mobitz2.pr: Constant on all conducted beats — no lengthening before the drop
mobitz2.qrs: Narrow or wide (often wide — the disease is infranodal)
mobitz2.hallmark: PR steady, steady, steady — then a P with no QRS, out of nowhere. No warning stretch. This is the dangerous 2nd-degree block.
mobitz2.mnemonic: "If some Ps just don't get THROUGH, then you have a MOBITZ II."
mobitz2.confuse: Wenckebach (PR lengthens first). If the PR never varies and beats still vanish, it's Mobitz II.
mobitz2.action: High risk of progressing to complete block. Atropine often fails (infranodal) — move early to transcutaneous pacing, expert consult, transvenous pacing.

third_degree.label: 3rd-degree (complete) AV block
third_degree.rate: Atrial ≈ 100 marching alone; ventricular escape ≈ 30–45, also alone
third_degree.regular: Ps regular, QRSs regular — but at different rates, unrelated
third_degree.p: Present and marching through everything, sometimes buried in QRS/T
third_degree.pr: Random — no two alike, because Ps and QRSs are strangers
third_degree.hallmark: Two independent rhythms sharing one strip. March out the Ps: perfectly regular. March out the QRSs: perfectly regular. Line them up: no relationship at all.
third_degree.mnemonic: "If Ps and Qs just DON'T AGREE, then you have a THIRD degree."
third_degree.confuse: Mobitz II (there, conducted beats still have a fixed PR; in 3rd-degree no P–QRS pair repeats).
third_degree.action: Atropine unlikely to help — go to transcutaneous pacing early, epinephrine/dopamine infusion as a bridge, transvenous pacing/expert consult.
